data.title: We Don’t Think ASAS Makeen Real Estate Development and Investment's (TADAWUL:9640) Earnings Should Make Shareholders Too Comfortable
data.description:
ASAS Makeen Real Estate Development and Investment Company (TADAWUL:9640) posted some decent earnings, but shareholders didn't react strongly. We think that they might be concerned about some underlying details that our analysis found.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

Examining Cashflow Against ASAS Makeen Real Estate Development and Investment's Earnings

As finance nerds would already know, the accrual ratio from cashflow is a key measure for assessing how well a company's free cash flow (FCF) matches its profit. To get the accrual ratio we first subtract FCF from profit for a period, and then divide that number by the average operating assets for the period. This ratio tells us how much of a company's profit is not backed by free cashflow.

That means a negative accrual ratio is a good thing, because it shows that the company is bringing in more free cash flow than its profit would suggest. While it's not a problem to have a positive accrual ratio, indicating a certain level of non-cash profits, a high accrual ratio is arguably a bad thing, because it indicates paper profits are not matched by cash flow. That's because some academic studies have suggested that high accruals ratios tend to lead to lower profit or less profit growth.

For the year to December 2025, ASAS Makeen Real Estate Development and Investment had an accrual ratio of 0.52. Statistically speaking, that's a real negative for future earnings. To wit, the company did not generate one whit of free cashflow in that time. Over the last year it actually had negative free cash flow of ر.س238m, in contrast to the aforementioned profit of ر.س129.9m. Coming off the back of negative free cash flow last year, we imagine some shareholders might wonder if its cash burn of ر.س238m, this year, indicates high risk. Having said that, there is more to the story. We can see that unusual items have impacted its statutory profit, and therefore the accrual ratio.

View our latest analysis for ASAS Makeen Real Estate Development and Investment

Note: we always recommend investors check balance sheet strength. Click here to be taken to our balance sheet analysis of ASAS Makeen Real Estate Development and Investment.

The Impact Of Unusual Items On Profit

The fact that the company had unusual items boosting profit by ر.س74m, in the last year, probably goes some way to explain why its accrual ratio was so weak. While we like to see profit increases, we tend to be a little more cautious when unusual items have made a big contribution. When we crunched the numbers on thousands of publicly listed companies, we found that a boost from unusual items in a given year is often not repeated the next year. And, after all, that's exactly what the accounting terminology implies. We can see that ASAS Makeen Real Estate Development and Investment's positive unusual items were quite significant relative to its profit in the year to December 2025. All else being equal, this would likely have the effect of making the statutory profit a poor guide to underlying earnings power.

Our Take On ASAS Makeen Real Estate Development and Investment's Profit Performance

Summing up, ASAS Makeen Real Estate Development and Investment received a nice boost to profit from unusual items, but could not match its paper profit with free cash flow. For all the reasons mentioned above, we think that, at a glance, ASAS Makeen Real Estate Development and Investment's statutory profits could be considered to be low quality, because they are likely to give investors an overly positive impression of the company. With this in mind, we wouldn't consider investing in a stock unless we had a thorough understanding of the risks. Our examination of ASAS Makeen Real Estate Development and Investment has focussed on certain factors that can make its earnings look better than they are. But the reality is that when a company loses money each year, for long enough, its investors will usually take their share of those losses. Loss-making companies are always racing against time to reach financial sustainability, so investors in these companies may be taking on more risk than they should.

In contrast to all that, many investors prefer to focus on companies like Likewise Group (LON:LIKE), which has not only revenues, but also profits. In the last three years Likewise Group's earnings per share took off; so much so that it's a bit disingenuous to use these figures to try and deduce long term estimates. So it would be better to isolate the growth rate over the last year for our analysis. Likewise Group's EPS shot up from UK£0.0041 to UK£0.0053; a result that's bound to keep shareholders happy. That's a commendable gain of 29%.

It's often helpful to take a look at earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) margins, as well as revenue growth, to get another take on the quality of the company's growth. If you believe that share price follows earnings per share you should definitely be delving further into Likewise Group's strong EPS growth. data.description:

discoverIE Group Insider Transactions Over The Last Year

In the last twelve months, the biggest single purchase by an insider was when Independent Non-Executive Chairman Bruce Thompson bought UK£91k worth of shares at a price of UK£6.07 per share. That implies that an insider found the current price of UK£6.08 per share to be enticing. Of course they may have changed their mind. But this suggests they are optimistic. With the share price around €19.43, modest annual revenue and net income growth, and a recent gain of 25.2% over 1 year, the key question is whether Getlink still offers an attractive opportunity at the current valuation or if markets already reflect expectations for future growth.

Preferred P/E of 32.9x: Is it justified?

On the latest figures, Getlink trades on a P/E of 32.9x, which looks high when set against both its recent earnings growth and the current share price of €19.43.

The P/E multiple compares what investors are paying today to the company’s earnings per share. A higher figure usually reflects strong confidence in future profit strength. For Getlink, earnings are forecast to grow at 2.3% per year and below the 12% expected for the French market, which raises questions about how much future profit growth is already factored into that 32.9x multiple.

Against peers, the contrast is clear. The European Infrastructure industry average sits at 17.3x, so Getlink’s current P/E is almost double that. It is also well above an estimated fair P/E of 13.2x that the SWS model suggests the market could move toward over time.
